lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of gypsum wallboard, plaster, molding, statuary, architectural plasterwork, and other gypsum products that make repeat sales to external distributors, contractors, builders, or industrial customers, including recurring specification, custom-production, inventory, and delivery work embedded in those relationships.
lens.excluded_activities: Gypsum mining without product manufacturing, captive internal units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band. One-time product transactions that do not amount to recurring or repeat outsourced supply are outside the screened population.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is principally product sales to building-material dealers, specialty distributors, lumber yards, home-center chains, contractors, and other construction customers. Wallboard is commonly priced on a delivered basis and shipped locally by truck; eligible specialty producers may also earn repeat revenue through custom fabrication, specification support, scheduled replenishment, and job-linked delivery.

a.bridge: NIST describes AI uses across US manufacturing rather than measuring the wage-weighted task mix of gypsum-product firms. I map predictive maintenance, inspection, forecasting, scheduling, document handling, and design assistance onto this industry's maintenance, quality, planning, administrative, and custom-product work. Because calcining, forming, drying, cutting, handling, and transport remain physical, applying the broad manufacturing use cases may overstate substitutable wage share.
a.rationale: AI can reduce planning, inspection, documentation, troubleshooting, scheduling, inventory, and some design labor, but the core production and logistics process is equipment- and material-intensive and still requires operators, maintenance staff, and safe physical execution. [S2] [S3]
a.change_evidence: A representative occupation and payroll extract from gypsum manufacturers, paired with task-level time studies for planning, quality, maintenance, customer service, and administration, would materially narrow this estimate.
caveats: No source measures AI-exposed task share specifically for NAICS 327420.
The estimate may differ sharply between automated wallboard lines and labor-intensive architectural-plaster shops.

rho.bridge: NIST reports manufacturing-wide AI use cases and barriers, while Census reports AI adoption across businesses of different sizes. I translate those broad observations into five-year implementation for eligible gypsum manufacturers, discounting for legacy-line integration, fragmented operating data, capital needs, plant safety, product quality, and workforce skills. Broad adoption evidence may overstate implementation in smaller specialty firms with weaker data systems.
rho.rationale: Predictive maintenance, computer-vision inspection, demand planning, scheduling, and document workflows are implementable within five years, but legacy equipment integration, data quality, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and the need for reliable production constrain full deployment. [S3] [S4]
rho.change_evidence: A survey of eligible firms covering machine connectivity, MES and ERP penetration, sensor coverage, current AI pilots, implementation budgets, and measured pilot-to-production conversion would materially change the range.
caveats: The cited adoption evidence is not specific to gypsum products.
Firm size and plant modernization likely create a wide implementation gap within the lens.

e.rationale: Most establishments manufacture and sell physical products rather than provide a recurring outsourced service. Eligibility is concentrated in specialty, custom, and regional producers whose repeat customer relationships include specification, fabrication, inventory, or delivery obligations; large commodity wallboard manufacturers and transactional shops are unlikely to fit the lens. [S1] [S2]
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of lower-middle-market gypsum producers, coded from customer contracts and invoices for recurring external service obligations, would directly determine eligibility.
caveats: Public sources do not enumerate lower-middle-market firms by revenue model.
The boundary between repeat product supply and an outsourced service is judgment-sensitive.

s5.bridge: Eagle Materials documents a highly concentrated wallboard market, not the five-year control-transfer rate of eligible lower-middle-market specialty manufacturers. I use that structure as evidence that strategic consolidation exists while limiting the base because eligible firms are a small specialty subset and no observed owner-age or transaction cohort was found. Concentration can understate transfers in a fragmented specialty niche but can also overstate strategic buyer access for very small firms.
s5.rationale: A concentrated strategic-buyer landscape supports some acquisition demand, but there is no direct succession or eligible-firm deal-flow series. The range therefore reflects bounded transfer judgment rather than an observed annualized rate. [S2]
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial transactions tagged to gypsum products, combined with owner-age and succession interviews for eligible private firms, would materially move this probability.
caveats: The market-concentration source concerns US wallboard, not all gypsum products or lower-middle-market firms.
No denominator-matched control-transfer cohort was located.

q.rationale: Implemented savings should initially accrue to the operator, but delivered product pricing, concentrated customers, standardized wallboard, renewal bargaining, and producer competition create meaningful sharing and repricing pressure. Custom specifications, local service, freight execution, and smaller specialty runs can preserve more benefit than commodity wallboard. [S2]
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts, price-change histories, bid win-loss data, gross-margin bridges, and interviews with distributors and contractors about renewal and rebid behavior would materially refine retained benefit.
caveats: The public source describes a large wallboard producer and may overstate customer concentration for specialty firms.
No contract-level pass-through or post-automation repricing data was found.

d5.bridge: Eagle Materials reports recent US wallboard shipment contraction and links demand to construction cycles; USGS reports broad gypsum supply, consumption, and construction dependence. These are product-market indicators rather than a forecast of constant-price demand for the eligible service basket. I carry recent weakness into the low case, use roughly stable physical demand in the base, and allow recovery in the high case. Wallboard-heavy evidence may understate resilience of repair, remodel, and specialty niches.
d5.rationale: Gypsum products remain embedded in residential, repair, remodel, and nonresidential construction, but demand is cyclical. Recent wallboard shipments declined while repair and remodel provides a stabilizing source, supporting a wide range centered just below current quantity. [S2] [S5]
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast from the Gypsum Association or Freedonia segmented by new construction, repair and remodel, nonresidential work, and specialty gypsum products would materially change this ratio.
caveats: Shipment data can reflect inventory and capacity effects as well as end demand.
The service basket for eligible specialty firms is not separately measured.

o.rationale: Year-five demand still requires an accountable manufacturer to source material, run regulated physical processes, assure quality, manage inventory, and deliver products. AI can change how the operator works but cannot replace physical gypsum products; modest leakage can arise from substitute materials, customer self-performance of coordination, or vertical integration. [S1] [S2] [S5]
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and specification data quantifying substitution, direct sourcing, prefabrication, and insourcing by contractors or distributors would materially change the estimate.
caveats: The accountable-operator share is not directly observed.
Specialty plaster and commodity wallboard face different substitution and channel risks.

narrative.executive_view: The industry offers a real but bounded operational AI opportunity in planning, maintenance, quality, documentation, and customer coordination. The central limitation is lens eligibility: gypsum manufacturing is primarily a physical product business, and US wallboard is dominated by a small group of large producers. A credible target set is therefore likely confined to specialty or regional manufacturers with service-rich repeat relationships.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve predictive maintenance, defect detection, production scheduling, demand forecasting, inventory visibility, technical-document retrieval, and custom-product design. It is unlikely to eliminate the core need for kiln, line, cutting, handling, maintenance, quality, and delivery work.
narrative.value_capture: Operators can retain savings where customization, specification knowledge, service reliability, freight execution, and short runs differentiate the offer. Commodity products sold through concentrated channels face stronger customer sharing and price competition, so durable capture depends on a defensible service component rather than line efficiency alone.
narrative.firm_availability: Commodity wallboard is unusually concentrated, while public data does not identify the count, ownership profile, or transfer history of eligible specialty firms. The practical acquisition universe may be much smaller than the dataset firm count suggests.
narrative.demand_durability: Wallboard and plaster remain essential physical building inputs, and repair, remodel, and nonresidential demand diversify exposure. Quantity nevertheless follows construction cycles, and recent wallboard shipment weakness argues against assuming uninterrupted growth.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the eligible-firm denominator, owner succession, contract-level pass-through, and the difference between modern wallboard plants and smaller specialty shops. Commodity exposure, customer concentration, freight economics, energy costs, regulation, and construction cyclicality could erase benefits that appear attractive in a workflow-only analysis.
narrative.principal_driver: Persistent need for physical gypsum products and practical AI use cases in maintenance, quality, scheduling, and administrative workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Very few lower-middle-market manufacturers may satisfy the recurring outsourced-service lens, and commodity channels can force efficiency gains into price.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS 327420: Gypsum Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: Official industry scope, including establishments manufacturing wallboard, plaster, plasterboard, moldings, statuary, and architectural plasterwork.

sources.title: Eagle Materials 2026 Annual Report and Form 10-K
sources.supports: Wallboard manufacturing and distribution, construction-linked demand, delivered pricing, customer concentration, market concentration, capacity, recent shipment decline, input costs, and operating constraints.

sources.title: The Rise of Artificial Intelligence in U.S. Manufacturing
sources.supports: Manufacturing AI use cases in predictive maintenance, quality, forecasting, scheduling, document management, and design, plus implementation barriers.

sources.title: AI Use at U.S. Businesses
sources.supports: Current business AI adoption and expected-use evidence by firm size, used only as a cross-industry implementation proxy.

sources.title: Mineral Commodity Summaries 2026: Gypsum
sources.supports: US gypsum production and consumption, wallboard sales, construction dependence, synthetic gypsum supply, and substitute materials.
